title: The History of Chocolate
description: For questions 9 – 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Chocolate is (0) one of the world's most beloved treats, but its history stretches back thousands of years. The story of chocolate begins with the ancient Olmec, Maya, and Aztec civilizations of Mesoamerica, who cultivated cacao trees and used the beans to create a bitter ceremonial drink.||The Maya and Aztecs considered chocolate sacred and (9) used it in religious ceremonies and as currency. When Spanish conquistadors arrived in the Americas (10) in the 16th century, they were introduced to chocolate by the Aztec emperor Montezuma. The Spanish transformed the bitter drink (11) into a sweeter beverage by adding sugar and vanilla, making it more palatable to European tastes.||Chocolate remained an expensive luxury in Europe for centuries, consumed primarily (12) by the wealthy. The Industrial Revolution brought machines that could process cacao more efficiently, and chocolate became affordable (13) for ordinary people. In 1847, a British company created the first solid chocolate bar, and milk chocolate was invented shortly (14) after.||Today, chocolate is (15) a global industry worth billions of dollars. However, there are concerns about sustainability and fair labour practices in cacao production. Many chocolate companies are now working (16) to source cacao more ethically and support farming communities in developing countries where cacao is grown.

example.text: Chocolate is (0) of the world's most beloved treats.

questions.explanation: Simple past: 'used it in ceremonies' describes a historical use.
Preposition 'in' is used with centuries: 'in the 16th century'.
Preposition required after 'transform' in the structure 'transform X into Y'.
Preposition 'by' introduces the agent: 'consumed by the wealthy'.
Expression 'affordable for people' indicates who it is affordable for.
Temporal adverb 'shortly after' indicates a short time later.
Expression 'working to source' indicates the goal of the work.
